author: @organiclibrary
text: Timbuktu, in the middle of the Sahara, was once one of the world’s most famous places for learning. Its schools, like Sankoré University, taught subjects such as math, astronomy, medicine, Islamic law, history, and literature. Famous scholars like Ahmed Baba and leaders like Askia Mohammad I made sure knowledge grew and was shared with students from all over West Africa. People in Timbuktu wrote and copied books on science, medicine, law, and religion, saving hundreds of thousands of manuscripts even during wars and invasions. This learning influenced other regions, helped preserve Africa’s history, and is still remembered today through the work of the Ahmed Baba Institute and UNESCO.

author: @rositascarborough5751
text: This is so fantastic!! Are you doing a sequel? So much more to unpack here like 1) Mansa Musa inherited the throne from his uncle who instead decided to lead a second expeditionary naval journey to the Americas. This was at least 200 years BEFORE Christopher Columbus attempted the same thing 2) Mansa Musa is well known for his wealth and the wealth of the Mali Empire but more importantly his governance style. He was a progressive and visionary leader who believed in plurality, equity and decentralized power. Even though he was Muslim he refused to impose Sharia law and instead maintained s governance philosophy of spiritual, intellectual, cultural diversity. He invested massively in the arts, culture, scholarship and academia. 3) The current borders of the Sahel Alliance are an almost perfect overlay of the Mali, Songhai and Mossi borders 4) Ibrahim Traore’s lineage is a clan of elite military brigades who were founded under Sundiata Keita, the first Emperor or Mansa of the Mali Empire. This brigade was certainly in place during Mansa Musa’s time and now we have a direct descendant leading a revolution in the same region. History is a living reality.

author: @axhamzautomobils3609
text:
I remember one day I told someone that we Africans should create our own official language. He said it was impossible because it would take centuries to develop. In my suggestion, I wasn't thinking about our generation; I was thinking about how it could actually be achieved.
If I wasn't being naive. The plan was for the fact that since we have so many tribes, it would be difficult to choose a single dominant language as the official one. Instead, we could bring in conlangers to merge African languages into one unified tongue. We would then start teaching and targeting the little babies and they very young ones through books and education as they grow up. Eventually, this language would become dominant across Africa. This alone would help us believe we are truly one people.

author: @waltermawuli8451
text:
The expression “Mari Dzata” is a corrupted form of an Ewe phrase. The original expression is “Amea di Dzata,” which translates to “He looks like a lion.”

In those days, the Ewe people played a vital role in the rise of Kaita the Great as a powerful ruler. They were highly knowledgeable, deeply lettered, and renowned for their mastery of powerful spiritual arts. Their understanding of the unseen world made them both guides and protectors to kings. As trusted advisors, they stood at the heart of Kaita the Great’s success.

Through their art of divination, the Ewes are said to have granted Kaita the ability to become invisible. This mystical power allowed him to strike with the strength, courage, and dominance of a lion. Because of this, people often described him as “Amea di Dzata”—one who looks like a lion.

In honor of this reputation and the power it symbolized, Kaita the Great even adopted the lion as his totem, placing its image upon his throne.


“Mari dzata” is simple language corruption over time

author: @tuitionjrnl
text:
This is entirely my opinion, but I think this is a part of the story that often gets overlooked when we talk about why the West interfered in our politics.

Nkrumah leaned strongly toward communism, and this was at the height of the Cold War—a period when the United States was openly hostile to leaders with communist sympathies. For a newly independent country openly aligning itself with the Eastern bloc, global attention—both political and strategic—was almost inevitable.

In my view, Nkrumah may not have fully anticipated the level of pressure that would come with occupying that ideological space. His speeches, political posture, and international alliances all sent clear signals to the world about where he stood and the direction he intended to take.

Moves like these don’t exist in isolation, and they come with consequences—especially when the internal systems needed to support and protect such a political vision are still developing. Vision alone wasn’t enough. The foundation required to sustain it simply wasn’t there.

author: @rositascarborough5751
text: very very true what was said about the Asante opposition to Nkrumah. Why would they trade a 300 year old empire for a nebulous idea and borders constructed by colonial administrators? This cleavage still informs Ghanaian politics today and should be discussed openly and dispassionately. Should Nkrumah have reverted to a real confederacy which was how the region was governed successfully for 1000 years versus a top down republic with centralized control that has mostly failed? Should he have incorporated pre-colonial chieftaincy governance and enshrined it in the Constitution the way Seretse Khama did in Botswana that has contributed to their governance excellence. These are the questions we should be debating instead of pretending the fault lines don't exist or sweeping things under the carpet that are festering and rotting. The current Asantehene is very much re-asserting the authority of the chieftaincy institution and is no longer doing it quietly. We need to completely overhaul the 1992 Constitution because an all powerful Executive republic with a weak Judiciary and sycophantic Legislature has failed massively and paralyzed the country's progress. Real balance of power and checks and balances have always been how our ancestors governed themselves. Chiefs and Kings were de-stooled and impeached OFTEN. A powerful military kept the aristocracy in check..........a mercantile class built wealth and a functioning civil service equivalent. These are not ideas or concepts that we needed from the white man.

Honey has a slightly lower glycemic index, meaning it raises your blood sugar a bit slower than white sugar. But calorie-wise, a tablespoon of honey actually has more calories than the same amount of sugar — about 64 calories versus 49 

In moderation, honey can be a better option, especially raw or unprocessed honey, which has antibacterial properties and antioxidants. It’s great for soothing sore throats or mild coughs.

But the myth is that honey is a free pass to use more sweetener — and that’s where people go wrong. Whether sugar or honey, too much can lead to weight gain, insulin resistance, and dental problems.
